questions.question: A finance analyst is comparing cloud service models for a new workload. They want to understand the shared responsibility model and which party is always responsible for protecting the data, identities, and account credentials regardless of whether IaaS, PaaS, or SaaS is chosen.
options: The cloud provider is always responsible for data, devices, accounts, and identities because Microsoft operates the underlying datacenters that host the customer information
Responsibility for information shifts to Microsoft whenever the customer chooses a SaaS offering such as Microsoft 365, leaving the customer to manage only network controls
Responsibility for data, devices, accounts, and identities is split evenly between the customer and the cloud provider across all three service models in a fixed 50/50 arrangement
The customer is always responsible for their information and data, devices, and accounts and identities in IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS, even though other layers shift to the provider
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the cloud provider never owns the customer's data, identities, or accounts under the shared responsibility model regardless of service model. Option B is wrong because even in SaaS the customer keeps responsibility for their data, accounts, and identity governance; Microsoft only takes on more of the stack below. Option C is wrong because the model is not a fixed 50/50 split; the line moves by service model and certain duties never transfer. Option D is correct because data, devices, and identities are always the customer's responsibility in IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS even as other layers shift.

questions.question: A security team is migrating an application to Azure App Service (a PaaS offering). They are reviewing the shared responsibility model to decide which operating system patching and runtime maintenance tasks they no longer need to perform compared to running the same workload on Azure Virtual Machines.
options: With Azure App Service the customer no longer manages the operating system or runtime patching because Microsoft maintains the underlying OS and platform stack for PaaS service
With App Service the customer must still install monthly Windows Update packages on the underlying virtual machine using the Azure portal update management blade for the service
Azure App Service is an IaaS offering, so the customer keeps full responsibility for OS patching, antimalware updates, and runtime maintenance just like with Azure Virtual Machines
Microsoft only manages the physical datacenter for App Service, leaving the customer responsible for guest OS hardening, runtime upgrades, and middleware configuration day to day
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Azure App Service is PaaS and Microsoft manages the OS and platform runtime so customers focus on the app and data. Option B is wrong because customers cannot and do not install OS updates on App Service; Microsoft handles platform patching transparently. Option C is wrong because App Service is PaaS, not IaaS, and the OS layer is not the customer's responsibility. Option D is wrong because Microsoft's PaaS responsibility extends well beyond the physical datacenter to include the OS and platform runtime.

questions.question: A new IT employee is studying Zero Trust and wants to know the three guiding principles that Microsoft uses to describe the model. Which list correctly states the three core Zero Trust principles as defined by Microsoft's published guidance?
options: Trust but verify, segment the network at the perimeter, and rely on strong passwords backed by a centralized on-premises Active Directory domain controller for user accounts
Defense in depth with seven layers, encrypt all data at rest with customer-managed keys, and disable legacy authentication protocols across every Microsoft 365 workload tenant-wide
Verify explicitly using all available signals, use least privilege access with just-in-time and just-enough access, and assume breach to minimize blast radius and segment access
Block all inbound traffic by default, require company-owned devices for every sign-in, and centralize logs in Microsoft Sentinel so analysts can correlate alerts across the estate
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because 'trust but verify' is the older perimeter model that Zero Trust explicitly replaces. Option B is wrong because defense in depth and encryption are separate concepts; they are not the three Zero Trust principles. Option C is correct because Microsoft defines Zero Trust by the three principles: verify explicitly, least privilege access, and assume breach. Option D is wrong because while these are useful controls, they are not the three guiding Zero Trust principles in Microsoft's framework.

questions.question: An architect is hardening a SaaS application and wants to limit lateral movement if an attacker compromises a single user. They configure micro-segmentation, end-to-end session encryption, and continuous monitoring so that one compromise does not expose the rest of the environment. Which Zero Trust principle is most directly being applied?
options: Verify explicitly by validating each access request with identity, device health, location, and workload signals before granting access to the application or resource requested
Use least privilege access by combining just-in-time, just-enough access, risk-based adaptive policies, and data protection so users only get the minimum permission actually needed
Defense in depth by stacking the seven security layers, ensuring physical, identity, perimeter, network, compute, application, and data each provide an independent layer of control
Assume breach by segmenting access by network, user, devices, and apps, encrypting all sessions end-to-end, and using analytics to drive detection and harden defenses end-to-end
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because verify explicitly is about per-request signal evaluation, not segmentation and blast-radius reduction. Option B is wrong because least privilege governs the scope of granted permissions, not segmentation and lateral movement limits. Option C is wrong because defense in depth is a layered controls strategy, not one of the three Zero Trust principles. Option D is correct because 'assume breach' is the Zero Trust principle that calls for segmentation, end-to-end encryption, and analytics to limit blast radius.

questions.question: An instructor is teaching the defense-in-depth model and asks students which list correctly enumerates Microsoft's seven defense-in-depth layers in the order presented in the SC-900 learning content from the outside of the environment inward to the most sensitive asset.
options: Physical, identity and sign-in access, perimeter, network, compute, application, and data form the seven layers in the order presented in the Microsoft defense-in-depth guidance
Identity, perimeter, network, compute, application, data, and physical, with the physical datacenter listed last because Microsoft handles that layer for all cloud customers
Perimeter, network, identity, compute, data, application, and physical, recognizing that perimeter security is always the outermost protection in a modern Zero Trust deployment
Data, application, compute, network, perimeter, identity, and physical, working from the most sensitive asset outward to the datacenter that Microsoft secures for the customer
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Microsoft documents the seven layers in this exact order: physical, identity and access, perimeter, network, compute, application, and data. Option B is wrong because physical is the first (outermost) layer in Microsoft's model, not the last. Option C is wrong because identity sits between physical and perimeter in Microsoft's guidance, not after the perimeter. Option D is wrong because this lists the layers inside out, not in the order Microsoft presents them in the SC-900 content.

questions.question: A security engineer is reviewing controls at the application layer of the defense-in-depth model for a custom internal web app hosted in Azure. Which control most clearly belongs at the application layer rather than at the network, identity, or data layers as Microsoft describes them?
options: Configuring Azure network security groups and Azure Firewall rules to restrict inbound traffic to the web tier subnet from only approved corporate IP ranges and partner networks
Enforcing Microsoft Entra Conditional Access policies that require multifactor authentication and a compliant device before any user can sign in to the web application
Encrypting customer records stored in the backend Azure SQL Database using Transparent Data Encryption with a customer-managed key stored in Azure Key Vault for the workload
Storing secrets in Azure Key Vault and integrating secure development practices and input validation so the application code is free from common injection vulnerabilities
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because NSGs and Azure Firewall sit at the network layer, not the application layer. Option B is wrong because Conditional Access and MFA are identity-layer controls in the defense-in-depth model. Option C is wrong because Transparent Data Encryption protects information at the data layer, not the application layer. Option D is correct because secure coding, input validation, and secret management are application-layer controls in Microsoft's defense-in-depth model.

questions.question: A retail company stores customer credit card numbers in an Azure SQL Database and wants to ensure that if backup files or storage media are stolen, the contents remain unreadable. Which type of encryption does this scenario most directly call for?
options: Encryption in transit, such as TLS 1.2 used between the application tier and Azure SQL Database, which protects information while it is being transmitted across the network only
Encryption at rest, such as Transparent Data Encryption that encrypts the underlying database files so stolen backups or storage media cannot be read without the encryption key
Hashing customer card numbers using SHA-256 so the original card value can never be retrieved or used for purchases by anyone who gains physical access to the backup files
Client-side tokenization that replaces every credit card number with a random surrogate before the value is ever sent to or stored inside the Azure SQL Database service tier
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because encryption in transit only protects data while moving across a network, not stored backup files or media. Option B is correct because encryption at rest, such as TDE, encrypts stored data and backup files so stolen media cannot be read. Option C is wrong because hashing is one-way and would prevent the merchant from processing card transactions; it does not solve at-rest storage encryption. Option D is wrong because tokenization is a separate technique and not what the question's at-rest scenario is describing.

questions.question: A developer is choosing between symmetric and asymmetric encryption for protecting large volumes of bulk data inside a backup job, prioritizing throughput while still keeping the secret material protected. Which choice and reasoning most accurately reflects how these two approaches differ?
options: Symmetric encryption uses the same key for encrypting and decrypting and is generally faster, making it well suited for bulk data, while asymmetric is typically used for key swap
Asymmetric encryption uses a single shared key and is preferred for bulk data because the public key length makes it faster than symmetric algorithms such as AES on most platforms
Symmetric encryption uses a public and private key pair, making it slower for bulk data, while asymmetric encryption uses one shared secret key and is preferred for throughput
Symmetric and asymmetric encryption are functionally identical in Azure because Microsoft transparently converts between them inside Azure Key Vault depending on the operation type
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because symmetric encryption uses a single shared key and is faster, making it suited to bulk data, while asymmetric is normally used to exchange that key. Option B is wrong because asymmetric uses a key pair, not a single shared key, and is slower than symmetric for bulk data. Option C is wrong because it inverts the definitions; symmetric uses one key and asymmetric uses a key pair. Option D is wrong because the two approaches are not equivalent, and Azure Key Vault does not silently convert between them.

questions.question: A web application stores user passwords. The security architect insists that even an administrator with full database access must not be able to read the original password values, but the application still needs to verify that a user-entered password matches what was stored. Which technique fits this requirement?
options: Symmetric encryption with a shared key stored in Azure Key Vault, so the application can encrypt the password on submission and decrypt it during verification at sign-in
Hashing the password using a strong cryptographic algorithm so the original value cannot be recovered; verification works by hashing the new input and comparing the digest values
Asymmetric encryption using the user's public key to encrypt the password, with the private key kept by the application server so it can be decrypted whenever the user signs in
Storing passwords in a TLS-protected Azure SQL Database table, relying on encryption in transit between the application and the database to keep the password values confidential
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because symmetric encryption is reversible, so an administrator with the key could read the original password. Option B is correct because hashing is one-way; the original cannot be recovered, but the application can hash an input and compare. Option C is wrong because asymmetric encryption is also reversible; anyone with the private key can decrypt the password. Option D is wrong because TLS only protects in transit and does not prevent administrators from reading stored plaintext passwords.

questions.question: A help desk technician resets a user account password and then grants the user access to a specific SharePoint site. The technician is asked to identify which of these two activities corresponds to authentication and which corresponds to authorization in Microsoft's identity terminology.
options: Both resetting the password and granting access to the SharePoint site are forms of authentication because they together establish who the user is during the sign-in flow
Both activities are forms of authorization because the help desk is using a privileged role to perform them on behalf of an end user that does not have those permissions
Verifying the password at sign-in is authentication (proving identity), while granting sign-in access to the SharePoint site is authorization (deciding what the identity may do)
Granting access to the SharePoint site is authentication and verifying the password is authorization, because permissions are evaluated before identity verification in Entra ID
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because granting site access is not authentication; it is about what the user can do once identified. Option B is wrong because password verification is authentication, not authorization. Option C is correct because authentication proves identity (the password check) and authorization decides what the identity is permitted to do (the SharePoint access). Option D is wrong because it reverses the definitions; identity must be proven before authorization decisions are made.

questions.question: A bank requires customers to sign in with a password and then approve the sign-in using the Microsoft Authenticator app on a registered mobile device. Which two MFA factor categories are being combined in this flow, and why does that combination qualify as multifactor?
options: Something you know and something you know, since both the password and the Authenticator approval rely on the user remembering a shared secret used during the sign-in step
Something you are and something you have, because the password is treated as a biometric factor and the phone is treated as a possession factor in Microsoft's MFA model
Something you have and something you have, because both the password manager that stores the password and the phone count as possession factors under Microsoft Entra rules
Something you know (the password) and something you have (the registered phone running Authenticator), which is two different factor categories and therefore qualifies as MFA
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the Authenticator approval relies on the registered device, not a memorized secret, so it is not 'something you know.' Option B is wrong because a password is 'something you know,' not a biometric. Option C is wrong because a password is knowledge, not possession; both factors must come from different categories. Option D is correct because combining a password (know) with a registered device approval (have) uses two different factor categories.

questions.question: A user signs in to a Windows 11 device using Windows Hello for Business with a fingerprint sensor instead of a password. Which MFA factor category does the fingerprint represent in Microsoft's authentication factor model used throughout the SC-900 content?
options: Something you are, because a fingerprint is a biometric factor based on a physical or behavioral trait that uniquely identifies the individual presenting it at the device in use
Something you have, because the fingerprint is physically attached to the user's body and therefore counts as a possession factor in Microsoft's authentication factor categories
Something you know, because the user must remember which finger to use, which makes the biometric act like a memorized secret similar to a personal identification number code
A federated factor, because Windows Hello relies on Microsoft Entra ID federation to evaluate the fingerprint against a centrally stored credential database in the cloud directory
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a fingerprint is a biometric, which falls under the 'something you are' factor category. Option B is wrong because possession factors are devices or tokens, not biological traits. Option C is wrong because biometric scans are not memorized secrets. Option D is wrong because there is no 'federated factor' category, and Windows Hello stores credentials locally bound to the device.

questions.question: A user signs in to Microsoft 365 once with their Microsoft Entra account and is then able to access Teams, SharePoint, and a third-party SaaS app without being prompted again. Which identity concept best describes this experience, and what is its primary benefit?
options: Federation, because trust between two separate identity providers is the only way that a user can move from Microsoft 365 to a third-party SaaS app without entering credentials
Conditional Access, which improves usability by suppressing the second prompt for users who satisfy a policy that combines user, device, location, and application signals together
Multifactor authentication, which when satisfied once for the day allows the user to access all integrated applications without being prompted again during the same browser session
Single sign-on (SSO), which allows a user to authenticate once and then access multiple integrated applications without being prompted to sign in again during the same session
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because federation establishes trust between identity providers, but the experience described is SSO, which can use federation but is not the same concept. Option B is wrong because Conditional Access enforces policies; it does not by itself implement the sign-in-once experience. Option C is wrong because MFA strengthens authentication; it does not provide the sign-in-once-to-many-apps experience. Option D is correct because SSO is the concept that lets a user sign in once and access multiple apps without re-prompting.

questions.question: A partner organization uses its own on-premises identity provider, but employees need to access an Azure-hosted application owned by your organization without each employee receiving a new account in your Microsoft Entra tenant. Which identity concept makes this possible?
options: Single sign-on alone, because once SSO is configured on application the partner users can use their own credentials directly with no additional trust or configuration in Entra ID
Microsoft Entra ID Protection, which evaluates sign-in risk in real time and can grant temporary access tokens to external users from a partner organization that does not federate
Federation, which establishes a trust relationship between two identity providers so users authenticated by the partner's IdP can access your application without separate accounts
Conditional Access with named locations, because adding the partner public IP ranges to a trusted-locations list allows their users to authenticate against your Entra tenant
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because SSO alone does not establish a cross-organization trust; federation does. Option B is wrong because Entra ID Protection evaluates sign-in risk; it does not establish cross-IdP trust. Option C is correct because federation is the trust between two identity providers that lets users from one access resources in the other without duplicate accounts. Option D is wrong because adding IP ranges does not create a cross-organization identity trust.

questions.question: A multinational retailer must keep all data about its European Union customers stored physically within EU member-state datacenters to satisfy regulatory expectations. Which compliance concept most directly describes this requirement about the physical location where the data is stored?
options: Data sovereignty, which describes the principle that data is subject to the laws of the country in which it is physically located, often driving choices about hosting region
Data residency refers to the geographic location where an organization data is physically stored, often determined by regulatory or organizational requirements in policy
Data classification, the process of categorizing data based on sensitivity and impact so that appropriate protection and retention controls can be applied to each type of data
Data minimization, the privacy principle of collecting only the information strictly necessary for a defined business or processing purpose under applicable privacy regulations
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because data sovereignty is about which jurisdiction's laws apply, not strictly the physical storage location. Option B is correct because data residency specifically describes the physical or geographic location where data is stored. Option C is wrong because classification categorizes data by sensitivity; it does not describe storage location. Option D is wrong because data minimization is about collecting less data, not about where data is stored.

questions.question: A privacy officer at a European retailer must align its handling of customer personal data with a regulation that requires explicit consent, data subject access rights, and breach notification within 72 hours. Which regulation most directly matches this description?
options: The Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA), which sets safeguards for protected health information handled by covered entities and their business associates
The Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS), a contractual standard administered by the major card brands for organizations that process or store card data
The General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the European Union regulation that governs the processing of personal data of EU residents and imposes consent and breach rules
The Federal Risk and Authorization Management Program (FedRAMP), a US government program that standardizes security assessment and authorization for cloud services used by agencies
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because HIPAA is a US healthcare law, not the EU privacy regulation described. Option B is wrong because PCI DSS is a card-industry standard about payment card data, not general EU personal-data privacy. Option C is correct because GDPR is the EU regulation that requires explicit consent, subject access rights, and 72-hour breach notification. Option D is wrong because FedRAMP is a US government cloud authorization program, not an EU privacy law.

questions.question: An attacker sends a crafted email that pretends to be from the user's bank and asks the recipient to click a link and enter their credentials on a lookalike sign-in page. Which type of attack does this describe in Microsoft's threat terminology used in SC-900?
options: Phishing, a social engineering attack in which the attacker sends fraudulent communications that appear to come from a reputable source to trick users into revealing information
Ransomware, a class of malware that encrypts files on the victim's device or network and demands payment in exchange for the decryption key required to recover the encrypted data
Distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), a coordinated network-flooding attack that overwhelms an application or service with traffic from many sources so users cannot connect
A supply chain attack, in which the attacker compromises a trusted vendor or software update mechanism in order to deliver malicious code to that vendor's downstream customers
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a deceptive message that impersonates a trusted brand to steal credentials is a phishing attack. Option B is wrong because ransomware encrypts files and demands payment; it is not a credential-harvesting email. Option C is wrong because DDoS floods a service with traffic; it does not collect credentials through email. Option D is wrong because a supply chain attack abuses a trusted vendor, not a fake bank email aimed at a single user.

questions.question: A company's file servers are suddenly inaccessible and every shared document has been renamed with an unfamiliar extension. A note on each desktop demands cryptocurrency in exchange for recovering the files. Which threat type is most clearly being described in this incident?
options: Ransomware, a type of malware that encrypts files or systems and demands payment, often in cryptocurrency, from the victim in exchange for the decryption key needed for recovery
Password spray, an attack that tries a single common password against many accounts in order to avoid lockouts, and which leaves files intact but signs in as a legitimate user
A distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack, where attackers flood a network or service with traffic from many sources to make it unavailable to legitimate users of the service
A man-in-the-middle attack, where the attacker silently intercepts and possibly modifies communications between two endpoints to steal or alter sensitive data flowing between them
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because encrypted files plus a cryptocurrency ransom demand is the textbook description of a ransomware incident. Option B is wrong because password spray is about credential guessing and does not encrypt files. Option C is wrong because DDoS makes services unavailable through traffic floods; it does not encrypt files for ransom. Option D is wrong because man-in-the-middle attacks intercept traffic; they do not encrypt files and demand payment.

questions.question: An attacker compromises a popular third-party software update server and inserts malicious code into a legitimate signed update. That update is then distributed automatically to thousands of customers who trust the vendor. Which threat category best describes this scenario in SC-900 terminology?
options: Phishing, because the attacker is impersonating the trusted software vendor to manipulate end users into installing software they would not otherwise have installed
Password spray, because compromising the build server typically begins with attempting one common password against many engineering accounts that have access to the update pipeline
Supply chain attack, in which the attacker compromises a trusted third party such as a vendor or update mechanism to reach the downstream customers who depend on that supplier
Distributed denial-of-service, because the malicious update is distributed widely to many customer endpoints at same time, overwhelming their ability to defend against it locally
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because phishing targets users with deceptive messages; it does not describe tampering with a trusted update pipeline. Option B is wrong because password spray describes a specific credential-guessing technique, not the broader trust abuse described. Option C is correct because compromising a trusted vendor's update mechanism to reach its customers is a classic supply chain attack. Option D is wrong because DDoS floods networks with traffic; it does not describe malicious code in a software update.

questions.question: Microsoft Entra detects thousands of sign-in attempts that each use a single common password but try many different user accounts, instead of trying many passwords against one account. Which attack pattern is this, and how does it evade lockout protections?
options: Brute force attack against a single user, which repeatedly tries many different passwords against one targeted account until it succeeds and is typically blocked by account lockout
Password spray, where attackers try one common password across many accounts to avoid the account lockout thresholds that would otherwise be triggered by repeated failed sign-ins
Pass-the-hash attack, where the attacker steals an NTLM password hash from one endpoint and then replays it across the environment to authenticate as the user on other systems
Distributed denial-of-service against the sign-in endpoint, where many requests are sent only to exhaust authentication capacity and not to actually compromise any specific account
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because brute force targets one account with many passwords; the scenario is the opposite. Option B is correct because spraying one common password across many accounts is exactly password spray, and it avoids per-account lockout thresholds. Option C is wrong because pass-the-hash reuses stolen hashes, not common password guesses. Option D is wrong because the goal here is account compromise, not service unavailability through traffic floods.

questions.question: A finance team is choosing a cloud service model where Microsoft is responsible for almost every layer of the stack and the customer is mainly responsible for users, devices, accounts, and information. Which service model fits this responsibility split most closely?
options: Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), where Microsoft only manages the physical datacenter, network, and hosts while the customer manages the OS and all software above it
Software as a Service (SaaS), where Microsoft manages nearly the entire stack and the customer is mainly responsible for their data, devices, accounts, and identity governance
Platform as a Service (PaaS), where Microsoft manages the operating system and runtime but the customer is fully responsible for installing and patching middleware and databases
On-premises, where the customer keeps full responsibility for every layer from physical hardware through application and data, with no Microsoft management of any stack layer
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because IaaS leaves the OS and software stack with the customer; Microsoft does not manage almost the entire stack. Option B is correct because in SaaS Microsoft manages nearly the whole stack and the customer focuses on data, devices, accounts, and identities. Option C is wrong because PaaS still leaves middleware and applications partially or fully to the customer; the scenario described matches SaaS better. Option D is wrong because on-premises means the customer manages everything; Microsoft is not managing the stack.

questions.question: An organization wants its security team to issue digital certificates internally to authenticate servers and users. They are explaining to leadership what a digital certificate is and how it relates to asymmetric cryptography. Which statement best describes a digital certificate?
options: A digital certificate is a symmetric key shared between two endpoints that allows them to encrypt traffic between each other without involving any external certificate authority
A digital certificate is the private key of a server published to clients so they can verify the server's identity by attempting to decrypt traffic with that same private key
A digital certificate binds a public key to an identity (like a server or user) and is signed by a trusted certificate authority so relying parties can verify identity and key
A digital certificate is an encrypted password file stored on a smart card the operating system reads during sign-in to authenticate the user without needing an identity provider
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because certificates carry an identity and a public key, not a shared symmetric secret. Option B is wrong because private keys are never published; certificates contain the public key, not the private key. Option C is correct because a digital certificate binds a public key to an identity and is signed by a trusted certificate authority. Option D is wrong because certificates are not password files; they are signed bindings of identity to public key.

questions.question: A SOC analyst is comparing the Zero Trust principle 'least privilege' to traditional permanent administrative access. Which combination of techniques best implements least privilege as Microsoft describes the principle in SC-900 study materials and Zero Trust guidance?
options: Granting permanent Global Admin rights to a small group of trusted engineers, because limiting the number of admins to a handful is sufficient to satisfy the least privilege idea
Allowing every user to keep standing Owner rights on their own Azure subscription so that no help desk involvement is required, which speeds up day-to-day operations across teams
Disabling Conditional Access for trusted internal networks so that administrators are not slowed down when working from the corporate office or VPN, which speeds up admin actions
Combining just-in-time elevation, just-enough access for specific task, and risk-based adaptive policies so users only have the minimum permissions required for the job at hand
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because permanent Global Administrator rights are the opposite of just-in-time, just-enough access. Option B is wrong because standing Owner rights for all users grants excessive standing privileges, not least privilege. Option C is wrong because disabling Conditional Access in trusted networks reintroduces implicit trust, which Zero Trust rejects. Option D is correct because just-in-time, just-enough, and adaptive policies together implement the least privilege Zero Trust principle.

questions.question: An auditor asks why Microsoft considers 'identity' to be the new security perimeter in modern environments instead of the network perimeter. Which explanation best reflects Microsoft's reasoning as presented in SC-900 learning content about identity?
options: Because the corporate network perimeter is now fully encrypted with TLS 1.3 end-to-end, the network firewall has become redundant and identity is the only remaining control plane
Because legacy on-premises Active Directory has been retired across customers, leaving Microsoft Entra ID as the only place where any authentication signals are evaluated at all
Because all attacks now originate from inside the corporate network rather than from public internet, making external perimeter controls less valuable than internal segmentation
Because users, devices, and applications work from many locations and networks, the consistent control point is the user identity, which becomes the primary perimeter to secure
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because firewalls are still useful; TLS does not make perimeter controls redundant, and Microsoft does not justify identity as perimeter that way. Option B is wrong because Active Directory has not been retired and is not Microsoft's reasoning. Option C is wrong because Microsoft's reasoning is about distributed users and resources, not that all attacks come from inside the network. Option D is correct because distributed users, devices, and apps make identity the consistent control point and primary perimeter.

questions.question: A new student is asked which of the following is an example of an identity provider (IdP) in Microsoft's identity terminology used throughout SC-900. Which single answer is the clearest example of an identity provider?
options: Microsoft Entra ID, a cloud-based identity provider that authenticates users and devices and issues security tokens that applications can use to authorize access to resources
Microsoft Intune, a unified endpoint management service that enforces device compliance policies and pushes configuration profiles, apps, and updates to managed user devices
Microsoft Defender for Cloud, a cloud security posture management and workload protection product that assesses Azure resources for misconfigurations and surfaces security findings
Sentinel, a cloud-native SIEM and SOAR platform that collects security signals from many sources and helps analysts investigate, hunt threats, and orchestrate incident responses
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Microsoft Entra ID authenticates users and issues security tokens, which is the definition of an identity provider. Option B is wrong because Intune is an endpoint management service, not an identity provider. Option C is wrong because Defender for Cloud is a posture and workload protection service, not an identity provider. Option D is wrong because Sentinel is a SIEM/SOAR platform; it does not authenticate users as an IdP.

questions.question: A compliance officer is explaining GRC concepts to a new analyst. They want a definition that clearly distinguishes governance from risk management and compliance as the three pillars Microsoft uses in SC-900 content. Which statement most accurately defines governance in this context?
options: Governance is the process of identifying threats, estimating their likelihood and impact, and applying mitigating controls so residual exposure to the organization is acceptable
Governance is the set of activities focused on adhering to laws, regulations, and standards such as GDPR, HIPAA, and ISO 27001 by enforcing controls and producing audit evidence
Governance is monitoring sign-in logs in Entra ID and reviewing Conditional Access policy outcomes to detect risky users and risky sign-ins in near real time on a daily basis
Governance is the system of policies, processes, and controls that direct and oversee how an organization operates, including how it manages information assets and security risks
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because identifying threats and applying mitigation describes risk management, not governance. Option B is wrong because adherence to laws and regulations describes compliance, not governance. Option C is wrong because monitoring sign-ins is operational identity protection, not governance. Option D is correct because governance is the system of policies and oversight that directs how an organization operates and manages risk.

questions.question: An administrator is explaining the role of directory services in identity management. They specifically need to describe what Active Directory Domain Services (AD DS) does on-premises. Which description most accurately reflects AD DS as covered in SC-900 study materials?
options: AD DS is a cloud-only identity provider operated by Microsoft that authenticates users to SaaS applications by issuing OpenID Connect tokens directly from Entra ID service
AD DS is a public key infrastructure service issuing, renewing, and revoking digital certificates for users and devices joined to an Azure subscription via Azure Key Vault directly
AD DS is an on-prem directory service that stores information about users, computers, and other objects and provides authentication and authorization within a Windows domain
AD DS is an endpoint protection product that scans Windows devices for malware and reports detections back to Microsoft Defender for Endpoint in Microsoft Defender portal directly
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because AD DS is on-premises, not the cloud-only Entra ID service. Option B is wrong because AD DS is not a certificate authority; Active Directory Certificate Services is a different role. Option C is correct because AD DS is the on-premises directory service that stores objects and authenticates/authorizes within a Windows domain. Option D is wrong because AD DS is not an endpoint protection product.

questions.question: An attacker uses a stolen username and password to sign in from an unusual location. Microsoft Entra evaluates the sign-in and decides to prompt for an additional factor based on the risk. Which Zero Trust principle does this real-time evaluation most directly reflect in the sign-in flow?
options: Least privilege access, because the additional factor reduces the standing permissions of the user and limits what the user can do after they have completed the sign-in process
Verify explicitly, because the sign-in decision is being made by evaluating identity, location, and risk signals at the moment of sign-in access not trusting the credentials alone
Defense in depth, because the additional factor is a separate physical layer (network) wrapped around the identity layer to provide an independent control over the sign-in flow
Assume breach, because the system is treating the network as fully compromised and using segmentation rather than authentication factors to limit the blast radius of the attack
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because requesting another factor is per-request evaluation, not a least-privilege scope decision. Option B is correct because evaluating identity, location, and risk to decide whether to challenge is the 'verify explicitly' principle. Option C is wrong because the scenario is identity evaluation, not adding a separate defense-in-depth layer. Option D is wrong because assume breach focuses on segmentation and blast-radius reduction, not real-time sign-in risk evaluation.

questions.question: A team wants a strong, modern second factor that is resistant to phishing because the second factor never travels over the network and is bound cryptographically to the legitimate site. Which authentication option best matches this description from Microsoft's authentication methods guidance?
options: An SMS text message with a one-time code, because the code is delivered to the user's mobile phone over the carrier network, which makes it inherently resistant to phishing attacks
A FIDO2 security key that uses public key cryptography bound to the relying party origin, so the credential cannot be replayed against a phishing site that mimics the real one
A shared password and a security question, because the security question acts as a second factor the attacker would have to know in addition to the password to sign in successfully
Email one-time passcodes sent to a personal mailbox, because email is end-to-end encrypted and cannot be intercepted between the sender and the recipient by an attacker in transit
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because SMS codes can be phished, intercepted, or SIM-swapped; they are not phish-resistant. Option B is correct because FIDO2 keys use origin-bound public key cryptography and the credential never leaves the device, making them phish-resistant. Option C is wrong because passwords plus security questions are both 'something you know' and are not phish-resistant. Option D is wrong because email codes are not phish-resistant and email is not universally end-to-end encrypted.

questions.question: A company's website is suddenly unreachable for legitimate users because traffic from millions of compromised devices is flooding the public endpoint at the same time. Which threat type and Azure-aligned mitigation pairing most accurately matches this scenario?
options: Ransomware mitigated by enabling Azure Backup with immutable storage policies and offline copies so the encrypted files can be restored quickly without paying any ransom demand
Phishing mitigated by enabling Microsoft Defender for Office 365 anti-phishing policies using mailbox intelligence and impersonation protection to filter messages before delivery
A distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attack mitigated by Azure DDoS Protection, which monitors network traffic and applies always-on mitigation to absorb volumetric attacks
A supply chain attack mitigated by code signing and verification of third-party update packages so any tampered or malicious update is rejected before it can be installed and run
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the scenario is service flooding, not file encryption that would call for Azure Backup. Option B is wrong because the scenario is volumetric flooding, not an email-based credential attack. Option C is correct because flooding from many sources to make a service unavailable is DDoS, which Azure DDoS Protection mitigates. Option D is wrong because the scenario is direct traffic flood, not a malicious software update from a trusted vendor.

questions.question: An organization is evaluating the difference between encryption and hashing for a particular workflow. They need to choose the technique that is one-way (cannot be reversed) and is therefore commonly used for password storage and integrity checks. Which description matches that requirement?
options: Symmetric encryption, because the same key is used to encrypt and decrypt, which means the output is one-way and the original input cannot be recovered without the encryption key
Asymmetric encryption, because the key pair (public and private) means that data encrypted with public key cannot ever be decrypted again by anyone, making it a one-way function
TLS encryption, because traffic encrypted with TLS between two endpoints is one-way and cannot be decrypted by any third party, including the original sender of message in transit
Hashing, which converts input data into a fixed-length value using a one-way function so the original input cannot be recovered, making it useful for passwords and integrity
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because symmetric encryption is reversible with the key; it is not one-way. Option B is wrong because asymmetric encryption is reversible with the private key; it is not a one-way function. Option C is wrong because TLS is reversible decryption at the endpoints; the sender can also decrypt. Option D is correct because hashing is a one-way function used for password storage and integrity verification.

questions.question: A bank uses TLS 1.2 between its mobile app and its backend APIs to prevent eavesdropping when a customer transmits account information over public Wi-Fi networks. Which encryption category does this scenario primarily describe in Microsoft's data protection terminology?
options: Encryption at rest, because the customer's account information is being stored inside the mobile app's local cache on the device while it is being submitted to the backend service
Hashing, because TLS protects the integrity of the data by producing a fixed-length digest of the request that the server can use to verify nothing was changed during transmission
Encryption in transit, because TLS protects data while it is being transmitted across networks between the client and server, preventing eavesdropping or tampering along the way
Tokenization, because TLS substitutes the original account information with a random surrogate value before the data is sent to the backend, preserving the original numbers safely
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because at-rest encryption protects stored data, not data moving over the network. Option B is wrong because while TLS provides integrity, the scenario described is about confidentiality of data on the wire, which is encryption in transit. Option C is correct because TLS protects data while it is being transmitted across a network, which is encryption in transit. Option D is wrong because TLS does not replace values with tokens; tokenization is a separate technique.

questions.question: A government tenant is concerned that even if its data is physically stored within a particular country, foreign laws could still apply to the cloud provider that operates the datacenter. Which compliance concept most directly captures the legal-jurisdiction aspect rather than the storage-location aspect?
options: Data residency, which describes the geographic location where data is physically stored and is the main way organizations satisfy a regulation that mandates storage inside a region
Data classification, the process of categorizing information by sensitivity such as public, internal, confidential, or highly confidential so the right protection controls apply
Data minimization, the privacy principle of collecting and retaining only the data needed for the defined purpose, often discussed alongside purpose limitation under GDPR rules
Data sovereignty, which describes how data is subject to laws and regulations of the country or jurisdiction in which it is located, including those that affect the cloud provider
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because data residency addresses physical storage location, not the legal jurisdiction angle the scenario is asking about. Option B is wrong because classification is about sensitivity categories, not legal jurisdiction. Option C is wrong because data minimization is a privacy principle about collection volume, not legal jurisdiction. Option D is correct because data sovereignty is specifically about the laws and jurisdiction that apply to the data.

questions.question: A team is mapping common Microsoft Entra and Azure capabilities to the three Zero Trust principles. Which capability most directly supports the 'assume breach' principle as opposed to verify explicitly or least privilege in the Microsoft Zero Trust framework?
options: Conditional Access policies that evaluate user, device, application, and location signals at sign-in to allow, block, or require extra controls for each individual access request
Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) network segmentation combined with end-to-end encryption and analytics that reduce the blast radius of a compromised identity
Microsoft Entra ID Governance access reviews that periodically require business owners to attest that each user still requires the access they hold to a given application or group
Microsoft Entra password protection that bans common and known-bad passwords across the directory to prevent users from choosing easily guessed credentials in the first place
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Conditional Access at sign-in is a per-request evaluation, which maps to 'verify explicitly,' not assume breach. Option B is correct because PIM-style segmentation, encryption, and analytics reduce blast radius, which is the heart of the assume breach principle. Option C is wrong because access reviews trim standing access, which aligns with least privilege rather than assume breach. Option D is wrong because banning weak passwords is a credential-hygiene control, not segmentation or blast-radius reduction.

questions.question: A teacher asks a student to give an example of an authorization control rather than an authentication control. Which of the following options is the clearest example of authorization as opposed to authentication in Microsoft's identity model used throughout SC-900?
options: Assigning a user the Reader role on an Azure resource group so the user can view resources but cannot make any changes to the resources contained inside that resource group
Requiring users to approve a sign-in prompt in the Microsoft Authenticator app on a registered mobile device after they have entered their correct password on the sign-in page
Configuring Microsoft Entra password protection to ban common or known-bad passwords across the directory so users cannot pick easily guessed credentials during password resets
Setting up a Microsoft Entra federation trust with an external identity provider so users from that organization can use their existing credentials to access internal applications
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because assigning the Reader role decides what an already-authenticated user is allowed to do, which is authorization. Option B is wrong because Authenticator approval is a second authentication factor, not authorization. Option C is wrong because banning weak passwords strengthens authentication, not authorization decisions. Option D is wrong because federation establishes how users are authenticated by a trusted IdP, not what they can do.

questions.question: A user pairs a hardware token that generates a one-time code with their password to access a financial application. Which two MFA factor categories are being combined in this sign-in, according to Microsoft's classification of authentication factors used in SC-900?
options: Something you are (the password) and something you have (the hardware token), because Microsoft treats user-memorized values as biometric in modern passwordless flows
Something you know (the password) and something you have (the hardware token), which combines a knowledge factor with a possession factor and therefore qualifies as multifactor
Something you have and something you have, because both the password and the hardware token are issued by IT to the user as possessions, and therefore both fall in same category
Something you know and something you are, because the hardware token's serial number is considered an inherent characteristic of user under Microsoft's authentication factor model
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because a password is 'something you know,' not a biometric factor. Option B is correct because combining a password (know) with a hardware token (have) uses two different factor categories, which is MFA. Option C is wrong because a password is knowledge, not possession; the two factors must come from different categories. Option D is wrong because a hardware token is possession, not an inherent biometric characteristic.

questions.question: An IT administrator wants to give every employee a single set of credentials that they can use across multiple integrated applications, reducing the number of passwords to remember and the number of sign-in prompts. Which capability most directly delivers this outcome?
options: Single sign-on (SSO), which lets users authenticate once with one set of credentials and then access multiple integrated applications without being prompted to sign in again
Multifactor authentication (MFA), which needs the user to present two or more authentication factors and increases assurance but does not reduce the number of passwords that exist
Privileged Identity Management (PIM), which provides just-in-time elevation for admin roles so that users do not hold standing admin privileges to high-value resources
Microsoft Entra password protection, which prevents users from choosing weak or banned passwords across the directory but does not reduce how many separate prompts they receive
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because SSO lets a user authenticate once and then access multiple integrated applications without repeated prompts. Option B is wrong because MFA strengthens each authentication; it does not reduce the number of passwords or prompts across apps. Option C is wrong because PIM provides just-in-time elevation, not a sign-in-once-for-many-apps experience. Option D is wrong because password protection bans weak passwords; it does not unify sign-ins across apps.

questions.question: A privacy officer is reviewing Microsoft's privacy principles that guide how Microsoft handles customer data in its cloud services. They want to identify one of those principles as published by Microsoft and referenced in SC-900 compliance learning content. Which option best fits?
options: Microsoft sells customer data to third-party advertisers when the use is anonymized in aggregate, and discloses the practice in Microsoft Online Services Terms for cloud services
Microsoft retains all customer data indefinitely so that customers can always recover it after termination, regardless of any retention policy the customer configures in services
Microsoft commits principles like control, transparency, security, strong legal protections, no content-based targeting, and benefits to customers from data Microsoft processes
Microsoft reserves the right to use customer email content to train consumer advertising algorithms, with no opt-out for commercial customers, even when the data is encrypted
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Microsoft's privacy principles explicitly state Microsoft does not sell customer data and does not use it for content-based advertising. Option B is wrong because Microsoft honors customer retention configurations, including deletion. Option C is correct because control, transparency, security, strong legal protections, no content-based targeting, and benefits to customers are the published Microsoft privacy principles. Option D is wrong because Microsoft does not use commercial customer content for consumer ads.

questions.question: An organization is implementing the Zero Trust principle of 'verify explicitly' in Microsoft Entra. Which configuration most directly implements that principle by evaluating signals such as user, device, location, and risk for each sign-in to a sensitive application?
options: A Conditional Access policy requiring MFA and a compliant device when a user attempts to access a sensitive application, evaluating user, device, and risk signals on each sign-in
A Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management policy requiring administrators to activate the Global Administrator role on demand for a limited window of up to eight hours daily
A Microsoft Sentinel automation rule that closes incidents flagged as low severity after seven days when no analyst has investigated the alert during active investigation window
A Microsoft Defender for Cloud secure score recommendation to enable Defender plans on Azure SQL databases so database threats can be detected and surfaced as alerts in the portal
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Conditional Access evaluates user, device, location, and risk signals for each request, which is the verify-explicitly principle in action. Option B is wrong because PIM enforces just-in-time elevation, which aligns with least privilege rather than verify explicitly. Option C is wrong because closing low-severity Sentinel incidents is alert hygiene, not a per-request verification of access. Option D is wrong because Defender for Cloud recommendations cover posture and workload protection, not per-request access evaluation.

questions.question: A security architect is mapping common controls to defense-in-depth layers. They are deciding which control belongs at the 'identity and access' layer in Microsoft's seven-layer model rather than at the perimeter, network, application, or data layer. Which option fits best?
options: Azure DDoS Protection enabled on a virtual network so volumetric attacks against public endpoints are absorbed by Microsoft global mitigation capacity before reaching the workload
Conditional Access policies in Entra that require multifactor authentication and a compliant device for sensitive applications, evaluating each access request before granting
Transparent Data Encryption on Azure SQL Database protecting database files with keys stored in Azure Key Vault, so stolen backups or storage media cannot be read without the key
Azure Web Application Firewall on Application Gateway inspecting HTTP traffic and blocking common attacks like SQL injection and cross-site scripting before they reach the web tier
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Azure DDoS Protection operates at the perimeter and network layers, not identity. Option B is correct because Conditional Access governs sign-in and access decisions, which is the identity-and-access layer of the defense-in-depth model. Option C is wrong because TDE protects information at the data layer, not identity. Option D is wrong because a web application firewall inspects HTTP traffic at the perimeter/application boundary, not the identity layer.

questions.question: A new employee at Contoso needs a contoso.com sign-in account that is created and managed directly in Microsoft Entra ID, not synchronized from on-premises Active Directory. Which Microsoft Entra identity type best fits this scenario?
options: A cloud-only member user created directly in the Microsoft Entra tenant and managed from the Entra admin center, with no synchronization from any on-premises Active Directory.
An external guest user invited via Microsoft Entra B2B collaboration, typically for partners or vendors who authenticate against a different home tenant or provider.
A workload identity such as a service principal or managed identity, which represents an application or Azure resource rather than a person signing in to Microsoft 365.
A synchronized hybrid identity sourced from on-premises Active Directory via Microsoft Entra Connect, where on-premises remains authoritative for most attributes.
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because a cloud-only member user is created directly in Microsoft Entra ID and is the standard identity type for a new internal employee with a contoso.com account. Option B is wrong because guest users are external collaborators invited via B2B, not new internal employees. Option C is wrong because workload identities represent applications or resources, not humans. Option D is wrong because the scenario specifies the account is created and managed directly in Entra ID, not synced from on-premises AD.

questions.question: A security architect wants to enable Microsoft Entra Privileged Identity Management (PIM) so that Global Administrator role assignments must be activated just-in-time and require approval. Which Microsoft Entra ID license is the minimum required to use PIM for directory roles?
options: Microsoft Entra ID P2, which includes Privileged Identity Management for Microsoft Entra and Azure resource roles plus Identity Protection and access reviews.
Microsoft Entra ID Free, included with any Azure or Microsoft 365 subscription, providing core directory features such as user and group management and basic reports.
Microsoft Entra ID P1, which provides Conditional Access, dynamic groups, and SSPR with on-premises write-back but does not include PIM or Identity Protection.
Microsoft Entra ID Governance, an add-on that adds entitlement management and lifecycle workflows on top of P1 or P2 but does not by itself unlock PIM.
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Privileged Identity Management is a Microsoft Entra ID P2 feature; P2 is required to use PIM for directory roles and Azure resource roles. Option B is wrong because Entra ID Free does not include PIM, Conditional Access, or Identity Protection. Option C is wrong because Entra ID P1 includes Conditional Access and SSPR with write-back but does not include PIM. Option D is wrong because Entra ID Governance is an add-on for advanced governance features and does not by itself enable PIM.

questions.question: Contoso uses Microsoft Entra Connect to synchronize on-premises Active Directory identities. The security team wants users to authenticate against on-premises AD with no password hashes ever stored in Microsoft Entra ID, while still using cloud sign-in endpoints. Which sign-in method best fits?
options: Password hash synchronization (PHS), which copies a hash of the password hash into Microsoft Entra ID so authentication happens in the cloud against the synced credential.
Federation with Active Directory Federation Services (AD FS), which redirects sign-in to an on-premises federation server but adds infrastructure to deploy and maintain.
Microsoft Entra B2B collaboration, which lets external users from another tenant sign in to Contoso resources with their home account and targets partner access scenarios.
Pass-through authentication (PTA), which validates passwords directly against on-premises Active Directory via a lightweight agent and stores no hashes in Entra ID.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because PHS synchronizes password hashes into Microsoft Entra ID, which the scenario explicitly rules out. Option B is wrong because AD FS keeps validation on-premises but requires deploying and maintaining a federation farm, which the scenario avoids. Option C is wrong because B2B collaboration is for external users from other tenants, not internal users authenticating against on-premises AD. Option D is correct because pass-through authentication uses a lightweight agent to validate passwords directly against on-premises AD and stores no password hashes in Entra ID.

questions.question: An administrator needs to create a group in Microsoft Entra ID that grants Microsoft 365 service permissions and also provides a shared mailbox, calendar, and SharePoint site for a project team. Which group type should they create?
options: A mail-enabled security group created in Exchange Online, which can grant permissions but cannot be created or fully managed from the Microsoft Entra admin center.
A dynamic security group with a membership rule based on department, which automatically adds members but does not provide a shared mailbox or SharePoint site.
A Microsoft 365 group (also called a Unified group), which provides shared mailbox, calendar, SharePoint site, and Planner integration in addition to access management.
A distribution list created in the Exchange admin center, which is for email distribution only and cannot grant access to Azure resources or Microsoft Entra applications.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because mail-enabled security groups are a legacy Exchange-managed hybrid and don't provide a full collaboration workspace such as SharePoint or Planner. Option B is wrong because a dynamic security group automates membership but doesn't include shared mailbox, calendar, or SharePoint. Option C is correct because Microsoft 365 groups are designed for project collaboration and include a shared mailbox, calendar, SharePoint site, and Planner on top of access management. Option D is wrong because distribution lists are for email only.

questions.question: A security admin wants to grant a user the ability to reset passwords and manage MFA settings for users in the Sales department only, not the entire directory. Which approach best satisfies least-privilege in Microsoft Entra ID?
options: Assign the Global Administrator role, which provides full directory control including password and MFA management but is far broader than the requirement.
Assign the Helpdesk Administrator role scoped to a Microsoft Entra administrative unit (AU) containing only Sales users, granting password reset and MFA for that scope.
Assign the Security Reader role on the entire tenant, which permits viewing security configurations and reports but does not grant the ability to reset passwords.
Assign the User Administrator role at the tenant level, which can manage all users including resetting their passwords and MFA but exceeds the Sales-only scope.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Global Administrator grants tenant-wide control and grossly violates least privilege. Option B is correct because Helpdesk Administrator scoped to an administrative unit (AU) containing only Sales users grants exactly the permissions needed (password reset, MFA) limited to the right scope. Option C is wrong because Security Reader is read-only and cannot reset passwords or manage MFA. Option D is wrong because User Administrator at the tenant level is too broad; the requirement is limited to Sales, which requires an AU scope.

questions.question: An organization wants any sign-in to Microsoft 365 from outside its corporate IP ranges to require MFA, while sign-ins from inside the corporate office should not be challenged. Which Microsoft Entra feature most directly enables this?
options: Security defaults in Microsoft Entra ID, which provide a baseline of MFA and other protections but apply uniformly and cannot be scoped to specific corporate IP ranges.
Per-user MFA enabled on every account, which forces MFA on every sign-in regardless of network location and offers no modern way to skip MFA on the corporate network.
Microsoft Entra Identity Protection sign-in risk policies, which respond to risky sign-ins like atypical travel but don't let an admin define a corporate IP range to exempt.
A Conditional Access policy that requires MFA, with named locations defined for the corporate IP ranges and a condition that excludes those locations from the requirement.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because security defaults apply uniformly and cannot be scoped to specific corporate networks or IP ranges. Option B is wrong because per-user MFA forces MFA on every sign-in and is no longer the recommended pattern for location-based exemptions. Option C is wrong because sign-in risk policies respond to risky behaviors, not administrator-defined corporate IP ranges. Option D is correct because Conditional Access with named locations lets the admin require MFA from non-corporate networks while exempting the corporate location.

questions.question: A user has forgotten their Microsoft 365 password. The organization wants users to reset their own passwords without help-desk involvement, and the new password must be written back to on-premises Active Directory so on-premises sign-ins also work. Which combination is required?
options: Microsoft Entra ID Free with self-service password change, which lets users update a known password but does not allow them to recover access when they have forgotten it.
Per-user MFA combined with security questions registered in My Sign-Ins, which proves identity but does not by itself reset a password or write changes back to on-premises AD.
Microsoft Entra ID P1 or P2 with self-service password reset (SSPR) and password write-back configured in Microsoft Entra Connect to push the new password to on-premises AD.
Microsoft Entra Connect with password hash synchronization, which copies on-premises password hashes into Entra ID but does not give users a portal to reset a forgotten password.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Entra ID Free allows only self-service password change for users who already know their password; forgotten-password reset with write-back requires P1 or P2. Option B is wrong because MFA and security questions are authentication methods, not a password-reset mechanism, and don't write back to on-premises AD. Option C is correct because SSPR with password write-back (P1 or P2 plus Entra Connect configuration) resets a forgotten password and pushes the change back to on-premises AD. Option D is wrong because PHS syncs from on-premises to cloud, the opposite of write-back.

questions.question: An administrator wants users to sign in to Microsoft 365 without a password by approving a notification on their phone. Which Microsoft Entra method specifically provides phone-based passwordless sign-in via push notification with number matching?
options: FIDO2 security keys registered via the My Security Info portal, which provide passwordless sign-in using a physical hardware key over USB or NFC, not via phone push.
SMS-based one-time codes sent to a registered phone number, which act as a second factor alongside a password but are not considered a passwordless method.
Windows Hello for Business, which provides passwordless sign-in to Windows devices using biometrics or PIN tied to a TPM but is bound to the device, not a phone push.
Microsoft Authenticator app configured for passwordless phone sign-in, where the user approves a push notification on the enrolled phone and confirms the displayed number.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because FIDO2 security keys are a separate passwordless option using a hardware key, not a phone push notification. Option B is wrong because SMS codes are a second factor, not a passwordless method. Option C is wrong because Windows Hello for Business is passwordless but bound to a Windows device's TPM, not a mobile-app push notification. Option D is correct because Microsoft Authenticator passwordless phone sign-in is exactly the method that delivers a push notification with number matching and replaces the password entirely.

questions.question: Contoso has Microsoft Entra ID P1 and uses Microsoft Entra Connect with PHS. The security team wants Conditional Access to automatically trigger a password reset when a user's risk level becomes high. Which licensing change is required and why?
options: No change is needed because Microsoft Entra ID P1 already includes risk-based Conditional Access policies tied to Microsoft Entra ID Protection user-risk and sign-in-risk signals.
Microsoft Entra ID Governance must be added because automated password resets are part of lifecycle workflows rather than Conditional Access or Microsoft Entra ID Protection.
Microsoft Entra ID P2 is required because risk-based Conditional Access policies that consume Identity Protection user-risk and sign-in-risk signals require P2.
Microsoft Entra External ID must be added because Identity Protection only evaluates external guest users by default, and internal user risk requires the External ID tier.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because while P1 includes Conditional Access, the risk-based variants that consume Identity Protection signals require P2. Option B is wrong because automated password reset triggered by user risk is an Identity Protection / Conditional Access capability, not a Lifecycle Workflows feature. Option C is correct because Microsoft Entra ID Protection (the engine for user risk and sign-in risk) and the risk-based Conditional Access policies that use those signals require Entra ID P2. Option D is wrong because External ID is for B2B/B2C scenarios.

questions.question: A Conditional Access policy requires a compliant device when accessing Microsoft 365. The admin wants the policy to apply only to interactive browser and modern-authentication clients, not to legacy POP3 or IMAP that cannot satisfy modern controls. Which assignment best fits?
options: Set the client apps condition to legacy authentication clients only, applying the compliant-device control whenever a user connects via POP3 or IMAP from a non-Outlook app.
Set the client apps condition to browser and mobile/desktop apps using modern authentication, excluding legacy authentication so the policy applies only to modern clients.
Set the device platforms condition to Windows and macOS only, since legacy POP3 and IMAP clients always identify to Entra as a recognized desktop platform during sign-in.
Set the sign-in risk condition to High so legacy authentication clients are treated as risky and a separate compliant-device requirement is enforced via the risk engine.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because applying the compliant-device control to legacy clients targets clients that cannot satisfy it; the right pattern is to block legacy authentication separately. Option B is correct because the client apps condition lets the admin scope the policy to browser and modern-auth clients while leaving legacy authentication to be blocked by a separate policy. Option C is wrong because device platform conditions filter on OS, not protocol. Option D is wrong because sign-in risk is computed from behavioral signals, not from the authentication protocol used.

questions.question: An administrator wants to give a developer permission to manage only the Application Registrations blade in Microsoft Entra ID, without granting broader directory or security permissions. Which built-in directory role most closely matches this least-privilege need?
options: Cloud Application Administrator, which manages all enterprise applications, app registrations, and Application Proxy settings across the directory, including credentials.
Global Reader, the read-only counterpart to Global Administrator, which can view nearly all configuration in the directory but cannot create or modify application registrations.
Application Developer, the built-in role that allows a user to create application registrations regardless of the tenant-wide self-service registration setting.
Security Administrator, which manages security features such as Conditional Access, Identity Protection, and alerts but does not include create/manage on application registrations.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because Cloud Application Administrator is broader than required and manages all enterprise applications across the directory, including Application Proxy. Option B is wrong because Global Reader is read-only and cannot create or modify application registrations. Option C is correct because Application Developer is the targeted, least-privilege role that allows a user to create and manage their own application registrations even when tenant-wide self-service registration is disabled. Option D is wrong because Security Administrator focuses on security features.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes Microsoft Entra ID Protection's distinction between sign-in risk and user risk?
options: Sign-in risk evaluates how risky a user's overall account is over time, while user risk evaluates how risky a single sign-in event is at the moment of authentication.
Sign-in risk and user risk are the same value reported in two different portals and both reflect the cumulative risk score that Identity Protection maintains for a user.
Sign-in risk reflects the probability that a specific authentication request is not from the legitimate user, while user risk reflects the probability the account is compromised.
Sign-in risk is calculated only for guest users invited via Entra B2B, while user risk is calculated only for member users and is the value used by risk-based Conditional Access.
questions.explanation: Option A is wrong because the definitions are reversed. Option B is wrong because sign-in risk and user risk are distinct signals with different meanings and lifecycles. Option C is correct because Identity Protection defines sign-in risk as the likelihood that a given authentication attempt is not the legitimate user, and user risk as the likelihood that the account itself is compromised over time. Option D is wrong because both signals apply broadly within Identity Protection's scope, not only to guests for sign-in risk.

questions.question: An organization uses Microsoft Entra Connect with PHS. The team wants users on domain-joined corporate computers to silently authenticate to Microsoft 365 in the browser without an additional prompt. Which feature provides that experience?
